title: Procalcitonin: positive acute-phase reactant
vignette: A 73-year-old woman comes to the emergency department with a 3-day history of worsening breathlessness and cough productive of thick, rust-colored sputum. The patient has a longstanding history of hypertension. Temperature is 38.3 C (101 F), blood pressure is 130/68 mm Hg, pulse is 102/min, and respirations are 24/min. BMI is 27 kg/m2. Jugular venous pressure is normal and there is no lower extremity edema. Heart sounds are normal with no murmur. Lung auscultation reveals left lower lobe crackles and bronchial breath sounds. Leukocyte count is 14,000/mm3 with 80% neutrophils and 7% bands, hemoglobin is 13.6 g/dL, and platelets are 400,000/mm3. Serum levels of which of the following are most likely to be elevated in this patient?

clues.note: Rust-colored sputum is classic for Streptococcus pneumoniae lobar pneumonia. It signals a bacterial process, which is the key to choosing a POSITIVE acute-phase reactant that rises with bacterial infection.
Tachypnea (24/min) with fever and crackles supports an acute infectious pneumonia. Vital-sign abnormality reinforces the inflammatory state.
Normal JVP, no edema, and no murmur deliberately exclude heart failure — steering you away from BNP. It's a 'rule-out' clue: this is pneumonia, not cardiac congestion.

Neutrophilia with a left shift (bands) indicates an acute bacterial infection. This bacterial picture predicts a rise in procalcitonin, which increases with bacterial toxins and falls with viral infection. WBC 14,000/mm3 (normal 4,500-11,000); platelets 400,000/mm3 (normal 150,000-400,000).

whatMatters: Strip it down: this is bacterial lobar pneumonia, and the question asks which serum protein RISES with acute inflammation — a positive acute-phase reactant. Procalcitonin is the answer (it rises with bacterial infection, falls with viral). Albumin, transferrin, and prealbumin are NEGATIVE acute-phase reactants (they fall); BNP is a heart-failure marker that's excluded here.
strategy: Sort the options into POSITIVE acute-phase reactants (rise: CRP, ferritin, fibrinogen, hepcidin, procalcitonin, serum amyloid A, complement) vs NEGATIVE (fall: albumin, transferrin, prealbumin/transthyretin). For a bacterial infection, pick the positive reactant; procalcitonin is the bacterial-specific one.

eliminations.label: BNP — heart-failure marker, excluded here
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: BNP rises with ventricular wall stretch in heart failure. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the stem explicitly rules out HF (normal JVP, no edema, no murmur); this is pneumonia, so BNP shouldn't be elevated. ✅ Correct if: the patient had dyspnea with elevated JVP, edema, and bilateral congestion (decompensated HF).

eliminations.label: cold agglutinins — Mycoplasma, wrong picture
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: cold agglutinins (IgM vs RBC I antigen) appear with Mycoplasma pneumoniae. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Mycoplasma causes a 'walking pneumonia' with dry cough, scattered/bilateral findings, and a NORMAL WBC — not rust-colored sputum with neutrophilic leukocytosis and lobar consolidation. ✅ Correct if: a young patient had dry cough, bilateral infiltrates, and hemolytic anemia.

eliminations.label: prealbumin — NEGATIVE acute-phase reactant (falls)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: prealbumin (transthyretin) is a nutrition/transport protein and a NEGATIVE acute-phase reactant. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: during inflammation the liver makes LESS of it, so it FALLS, not rises. ✅ Correct if: the question asked which protein DECREASES in acute inflammation (or tracks short-term nutrition).

eliminations.label: transferrin — NEGATIVE acute-phase reactant (falls)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: transferrin carries iron and is a NEGATIVE acute-phase reactant. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: in inflammation it DECREASES (mirroring functional iron sequestration via hepcidin); it doesn't rise. ✅ Correct if: asked which protein falls in the anemia of chronic disease / acute inflammation.

reasoning: Rust-colored sputum + neutrophilia with left shift = bacterial lobar pneumonia
Question asks which serum protein RISES (positive acute-phase reactant)
Procalcitonin rises with bacterial toxins, falls with viral infection
Other options are negative reactants or the excluded BNP → answer D
mechanism: Acute-phase reactants are liver-made proteins whose levels shift >=25% during inflammation. Positive reactants rise (CRP, fibrinogen, ferritin, hepcidin, serum amyloid A, complement, procalcitonin); negative reactants fall (albumin, transferrin, prealbumin/transthyretin). Procalcitonin specifically rises in response to bacterial toxins and falls with viral infection, so it helps distinguish bacterial from viral community-acquired pneumonia.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Pathology > acute-phase reactants: memorize POSITIVE (CRP, ferritin, fibrinogen, hepcidin, serum amyloid A, procalcitonin, complement) vs NEGATIVE (albumin, transferrin, prealbumin/transthyretin) and procalcitonin's bacterial-vs-viral utility.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Decode the vignette to 'bacterial infection' and the question to 'which protein goes UP.' Then split options into up vs down reactants; pick the positive, bacterial-specific one.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on acute-phase reactant, positive vs negative reactant, procalcitonin, prealbumin/transthyretin, left shift/bands? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Prealbumin and transferrin are real serum proteins but they FALL in inflammation; BNP and cold agglutinins are planted to test HF vs Mycoplasma. Anchor on 'rises with bacterial infection' = procalcitonin.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: The 28% national correct rate means this one's tricky — drill the positive/negative reactant lists until automatic.

keywords.front: Rises with bacterial infection, falls with viral

keywords.back: CRP, ferritin, fibrinogen, hepcidin, serum amyloid A, procalcitonin, complement

firstAid.detail: Pathology > inflammation: positive vs negative acute-phase reactants; procalcitonin for bacterial vs viral infection; ESR as an indirect marker.
firstAid.location: Pathology > inflammation: positive vs negative acute-phase reactants; procalcitonin for bacterial vs viral infection; ESR as an indirect marker.

firstAid.detail: Pulmonary/Micro > CAP: S. pneumoniae (rust sputum, lobar), typical vs atypical (Mycoplasma) presentations.
firstAid.location: Pulmonary/Micro > CAP: S. pneumoniae (rust sputum, lobar), typical vs atypical (Mycoplasma) presentations.

firstAid.detail: Cardio > BNP in heart failure; Heme > transferrin and hepcidin in iron handling / anemia of chronic disease.
firstAid.location: Cardio > BNP in heart failure; Heme > transferrin and hepcidin in iron handling / anemia of chronic disease.

memoryHook: ProCALCitonin CALLS out bacteria (up); albumin/transferrin/prealbumin DUCK down when the liver pivots to fighting inflammation.
